HMW-V09: WeChat Mini Program — Driver Job Access

How might we design the driver's job execution experience as a WeChat Mini Program — balancing WeChat-native UX patterns with Teleport OS status consistency, for a driver who may be at a warehouse loading bay with gloved hands?
Context (TODO-049): This introduces a 3rd app in the Teleport OS platform: Admin (web) → Vendor (web) → Driver (WeChat). When an FM Trucking job is assigned to a driver, the driver receives a WeChat notification and opens the job in a Mini Program. The driver needs: pickup/delivery info, cargo details, action buttons for status milestones (arrived at pickup, departed, arrived at delivery), and proof photo upload from their phone camera. Their updates flow back to the Vendor and Admin apps via the shared state layer. WeChat Mini Programs run inside the WeChat app on a phone, constraining font choice (PingFang SC/Noto Sans SC), screen width (375–430px), and interaction patterns. The mockups here approximate the WeChat UI using Instrument Sans (the closest web proxy). Production would use WeChat's system font stack.

Key design tension: Should this app follow Teleport OS design conventions (4–6px radius, Instrument Sans, dense utilitarian) or WeChat native conventions (8–12px cards, system font, generous touch targets)? Drivers are warehouse workers, not ops planners — comfort and speed at a loading dock take priority over visual coherence with the admin tool. However, status signal colors (blue/amber/green/red dots) must remain identical so dispatchers reading the same job in the vendor app see the same language.

Option A: Single-Scroll Card Layout + Sticky Action Bar

Recommended
One scrollable page with tightly organized cards: Status banner → Route → Cargo → Proof files. A sticky bottom action bar always shows what to do next, adapting per status milestone. The driver sees all job context on one screen and the primary action is always reachable without scrolling. The status banner uses Teleport OS colors (blue dot for In Progress, amber for Completed, green for Verified) so dispatchers in the vendor app and drivers in WeChat see the same signal. Cards use 8px radius (WeChat standard) rather than the admin's 4–6px — this is an intentional exception for the WeChat native environment. Shown below: the driver has arrived at the delivery point and needs to upload proof photos.
Pros
• All job info visible in one place — no navigation
• Sticky action bar = primary action always reachable
• Context before action: driver sees pickup/delivery details, cargo, and current status in one scroll
• Natural for both desktop preview and phone usage
• Camera action is always the same place (bottom bar)
Cons
• Route + Cargo requires scrolling to reach Proof section
• No forced progression — driver could upload proof without marking arrival
• Less opinionated about step sequence than a wizard

Option B: Step-by-Step Milestone Wizard

Simplest
Each status milestone occupies a full screen. The driver has one thing to do: press the big confirmation button. No scrolling, no navigation, no reading. The driver's current action fills the entire screen with a large touch target (>56px). Context about the delivery (location, time) appears in a compact footer strip. After confirmation, the screen automatically advances to the next step. Designed for maximum error-proofing: the driver cannot accidentally mark a wrong step, and the large button works even with gloved hands.
Pros
• Zero ambiguity — one action per screen, full-size button
• Glove-friendly: single 56px+ tap target
• Enforced step sequence prevents out-of-order actions
• Works in bad lighting (minimal reading required)
Cons
• Driver cannot see full job context (cargo, MAWB, ETA) on the action screen
• Multi-screen flow is harder to navigate if driver needs to go back
• "View job details" link is an escape hatch that requires extra tap
• Doesn't work well for photo upload — camera handoff breaks the wizard pattern

14:09
Jobs
DO-2603-001
Upload proof photos
HKIA Cargo Terminal
Delivery receipt + cargo stamp required
Design note: After "Take Photo," user enters the camera app and returns. The wizard pattern breaks here — multi-photo upload requires a different UI (file list + add more). Option A handles this more naturally.

Option C: Tab-Based Navigation — Job · Progress · Photos

Most organized
Three tabs organize information by type: Job (route, cargo, trip info), Progress (milestone timeline, status), Photos (upload and view proofs). A WeChat-style tab bar at the bottom matches the native navigation pattern that drivers use in dozens of other apps. The primary action button lives inside the active tab — no sticky bar, context-specific. Best suited for drivers who want to review trip details separately from taking actions. Photo upload gets a full dedicated screen.
Pros
• Cleanly separates information types: info vs. actions vs. proofs
• Photos tab gives full screen for upload — natural camera workflow
• Familiar WeChat tab bar — no explanation needed
• Badge on Photos tab can show upload count / pending state
Cons
• Action is buried in a tab — no persistent "what to do next" signal
• Switching tabs adds friction for the main workflow
• 3 tabs may be overkill for a simple pickup-drive-deliver loop
• Tab bar competes with WeChat's own bottom navigation

1
Photos

WeChat Mini Program Design Constraints

What stays identical to Teleport OS:
• Status dot colors — Pending #d1d5db, In Progress #152CFF, Completed #a16207, Verified #059669, Rejected/Cancelled #dc2626
• Status labels (Pending / In Progress / Completed / Verified)
• Data typography: JetBrains Mono for IDs, times, plates
• Accent #152CFF for all interactive elements (buttons, links, active nav)
WeChat-adapted (intentional exceptions):
• Card border radius: 8px instead of 4–6px (WeChat UI standard)
• Font: PingFang SC (iOS) / Noto Sans SC (Android) — system font, not Instrument Sans. Mockup shows Instrument Sans as proxy.
• Touch targets: min 44–56px (glove-friendly), larger than the admin's 28–32px rows
• Nav bar: WeChat native (back arrow left, title center, ··· right) — not customizable

✓ Recommendation: Option A — Single-Scroll + Sticky Action Bar

Why A over B (wizard): The wizard pattern fails at proof upload — after "Take Photo" the user goes into the camera app and returns, breaking the step sequence. Option B also hides all job context (cargo, MAWB, ETA) behind a "View details" escape hatch, which drivers need for reference while at the warehouse. Option A keeps context always visible, and the sticky action bar gives the same "one thing to do" clarity as a wizard without forcing navigation.

Why A over C (tabs): Three tabs add navigation overhead for a workflow that is fundamentally linear (pickup → drive → deliver → photo). The tab bar competes visually with the WeChat nav bar and creates ambiguity about where the primary action lives. Option A's sticky action bar is always present and always contextual — zero navigation required to act.

Key design decisions for Option A implementation:

1. Progress strip at top (not bottom): the 3-step progress strip (Picked Up → Departed → Delivered) should live in a card near the top, not in a tab. Drivers orient by "what step am I on" before reading route details.
2. Fade completed route points: Origin at 65% opacity once departed — keeps focus on the destination.
3. Status banner uses Teleport OS per-state tinted backgrounds: Pending=`#f9fafb`, In Progress=`rgba(21,44,255,0.04)`, Completed=`#fefce8`, Verified=`#f0fdf4`. Identical to the vendor app Status Action Bar spec — same signal, same color.
4. Camera button is primary action: At the delivery stage, the button says "📷 Upload Proof Photos" and opens the native camera (WeChat `wx.chooseImage` API with `sourceType: ['camera']`). Multi-photo supported.
5. Data flows back: Driver milestone taps and photo uploads append to `activityLog` with `user: driver.name` (e.g., "Zhang Wei"), same field the vendor/admin see. Proof uploads use the same `ADD_PROOF_DOCUMENT` reducer action — no separate driver-only data model needed.
6. Notification entry point: Driver receives a WeChat service message when assigned: "New job: Baoan WH → HKIA, 03 May 08:00. Tap to open." Deep-links directly to this job detail screen.

Open question for user: Should the "Arrived at Pickup" / "Departed" / "Arrived at Delivery" milestone buttons be part of the sticky action bar (sequential, one at a time), or should they be inline buttons within the Progress card that can be tapped in any order? The sticky bar enforces sequence; the inline buttons give flexibility for cases where milestones happen out of order at the warehouse.
